speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Rannie Johnson Vine, who is better known as Ma Vine, took place at her home in Elk River on July 8th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager. This moving to Montana, why did you move there in the first place?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, you know, they advertised that part of the country at that time like they do everything else and homesteading and one thing or another. So my husband and several other men with families that just took the notion of going to go out there and homestead. Three hundred and twenty acres of land, you know, sounded big because we came from Wisconsin. Wisconsin isn't too big of a state and it's all daring. My husband didn't want nothing to do with the cow. He said, I can't see. He said, however the world of them, a person's going to make a living. He said, I'm stripping a dime out of a cow every night and morning. So he didn't like it so they went on out there and we done all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he been daring in Wisconsin? Was the people that went out with you neighbors of yours?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, and a lot of his relatives, his father and mother came out with us and one of his brothers and my brother came out there with us. But he went back. He said, none of this for me. He went back to his farm in Wisconsin. He said, that's good enough for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And where in Wisconsin was this, were you there?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, do you know it, have you ever been down in there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A little bit. I've driven through it mostly.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Do you know where Chippewa Falls is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: It's, oh I don't know how far east of St. Paul but it would be probably a hundred and fifty miles or something like that. We were just about in the central part of Wisconsin. In a small town they called Greenwood. That was an old logging town too. Greenwood?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Greenwood was an old logging town. And when I was a child they, I heard them tell about, well, they're pulling the thumbnail out They'll be the last of the lumbering and woodwork around Greenwood. And they still are hauling in and they got little packets. They looked at the coal then now. It's mostly basswood. They make boxes for up in the northern part of Wisconsin for fishing boxes and stuff like that to put the fish in and a lot of fishing googling on off there. And they're still at that hauling in a few basswood bolts I think they call them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this decision to go to Montana, do you think that they, that your husband and others, that you all felt that you could better, do a lot better out in Montana than in Wisconsin?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, my husband did for this one reason. He didn't like that very much. He was born in a lumber camp in Wisconsin and of course that's what he liked. And when he got out into Montana he done all right. Although he wasn't a very good farmer. But then when the crops began to fail, why, I'm going out on the coast, out in the timber again. That's how I come out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you tell me a little bit about what it was like in, around Fort Peck when you got there? Was it at all developed already? No.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, Fort Peck of course. See, that's on the north side of the Missouri River and this homesteading that they had going was south of Missouri. So we had to cross the ferry to get on the south side of the river. And in 1910 there wasn't anybody, well there was just a few, very few people that started the homestead. But during 1910 and 11, why, there was cabins sprung up on most, every 320 acres all around there. So there was people from North Dakota that lived there and done okay I guess, I don't know. Anyway, they homesteaded. See, we homesteaded, I think it was 20 miles south of the Missouri River and the Missouri River as far by train as there and on the stop. That's right on the back of the Missouri there. And we homesteaded about 20 miles south of there. And then there was another project 20 miles further and there's any amount of people moved in there. And the second year we were there, 1911, why, there's a, I don't know what they were, I don't know. Some foreign, anyway, foreigners of some kind that either Hollanders or Germans or something like that that had formed in North Dakota. Well then they took up these homesteads, the old man did and his son was old enough to take up land and they took up an awful lot of it up in there. And they brought in their equipment from North Dakota, you see. Great big old tractors and plows and what you had. So, when they get as far as our place, this old fellow says, he says, I'd like to plow that field over there for you. I think it's something like 100 acres or something like that over there. And my husband says, I haven't got the money now, he says the paper. He says, I didn't ask you for any money. He says, let me plow that. And he says, if you can, he says, put it in the flax. And he says, I'll wait for my pay. I just don't want to wait until my home's sitting there. And we've got lots of buildings and things to do there and the boys can do that. So that's the way it happened. And I never saw such beautiful flax in my life. Just about this high and just as level and blue, just wonderful. We made all kinds of money out of that flax for two straight years. And that kind of put us on our feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you build your homestead cabin on the place?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, we shipped out two regular cars full of lumber and everything that we needed from Wisconsin. We had quite a nice little house on our plane. And people then, they were so different from what they are now. Now there's a carpenter that lived down on, close to the Missouri. And his land would be flooded every year, you know, by the jam up and then it would flood his land. So he couldn't do much of anything. So he done carpenter work for people that wanted to build one thing or another. So he come to my husband and we had lots of lumber on hand. And he says, let me build you a barn. He said, a good big one with a good hay loft. And we had some maple flooring with the stuff about this wide, you know. He said, put that in the loft. He says, and you said for a dance hall for a while. And he said, I'll bet you you have that paid for in just no time. So that's the way it happened. And we shipped a piano out with it. And we had that and so when he had the barn finished dry, he says, now let's give a dance. They advertised a little bit. So they did. I never seen so many people in my life, as the world come to that, from all cowboys, you know. Come for 150 miles from Miles City and all of those places. They didn't have food enough to feed them or anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have dances there very often after that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, big ones for Red Cross and one thing or another. We made an awful lot of money for the Red Cross with the heavy dances there. We didn't put no hay in there for a long time. I don't know if we ever put hay in there. You know, that prairie hay, you could stack that. And, oh yeah, we done okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the dance would, when you had a dance there, would it be often just a community dance just for local families?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, once in a while we'd have a, just for the neighborhood or something like that. I'll try to. I remember one time the neighbors said, let's have a little party and dance Saturday night. And I said, okay, I don't care. So we did. First thing we knew, I looked up and here was horses and cowboys and women and kids all over the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't hardly keep them away.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, yeah, that was advertised there for all, all around me. But there's a lot of work about it. Because I enjoyed it. And then there was quite a few people from Chicago that come out there and hosted it. And some of them had been in the baseball league since Chicago. And of course they started baseball. All 5,000 there. And that was every Sunday and every Sunday. I might as well bake up a lot of 20 loaves of bread and cook up a ham or two or something else and feed that bunch every Sunday. Enjoyed every minute of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they play at? Close to the home?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes. Let's see. Yeah. Here's our homestead. Here's my father-in-law's homestead. Here's my sister-in-law's homestead. And here's Hubert's. That's another fellow that comes and we knew that. So we had our buildings right close here. And this sister-in-law, she wasn't married yet to my brother-in-law, but anyway they were going to be. But she come out there and homesteaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She homesteaded?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: She homesteaded. Teacher, yeah. And my two oldest girls and three other children. She taught them. I'm sure that you would think she was out here last summer. Really?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Herself?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, she came from Wisconsin with us. She just got that urge that she wanted to come west. She taught school in our neighborhood in Wisconsin. And that's how she got acquainted with my brother-in-law. And she wanted to come out and she came out with us. And she had her homestead. Let's see. I got her whole history here. The whole business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she write it down?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: She wrote it. I just don't know where I got that. I haven't read it yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of hard work, too.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh yeah, but everybody worked hard every day. We didn't think anything about it. Different is now. Boy, if that work had to be done, you might as well get in and do it. Forget about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the hard work when you think about it now?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, as far as housekeeping, we always had so many hired men. Not many, I shouldn't say that. About eight or ten all the time. And we had to haul all of our water a mile and a half, drinking water, wash water, bath water, and the whole work. And then the water was so hard you had to break it with a lie. And the winters were severe back there. Sixty below, wind blowing. And, but people didn't think anything about it. We didn't know any better, I guess. You know, a lot of people now, you start to tell them that. Of course, the younger people don't, it's not interested in it in the least because it can't be sold. That's just all they could. And, but anyway, we never thought about that. You know, people this day and age, they feel like they got to be right close to a doctor all the time. Well, I raised my family out there on the hills, or in Montana, I shouldn't say hills, because it wasn't as flat as flat as it could be. But we never thought about a doctor. Never gave it a thought. We got along just fine. And it was during that, if you will, I said, in 1918, I believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're thinking that flu?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: When the flu was so bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, that was terrible. If any amount of people had died right out there on the prairie with that. Not any more so than they did in town because there wasn't anything much that a doctor could do for it. Mostly, the people that died was pregnant mothers. The temperature runs high. It killed them that way. Yeah, that was a bunch of them. It struck a neighborhood just north of us those little ways, and those people came from Canada and home-sitted in there. French people. They were hit awful hard. There were several of them that died. But, in a way, we helped one another and we got along just swell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any way of treating it? Was there any way of treating it that you used out there?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, we didn't have anything much to treat it with. And the doctor came up once or twice and I happened to... They all called on me for some reason, Mother. I don't know why. If the children were sick or anything, they'd just have to have me. I didn't know a thing about those things much. But, in a way, he'd give them shots, you know. And then they'd give me orders what to do. The temperature gets up so high, I'd give them a shot. I never did. I was supposed to, but the kids lived anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in general, what kind of remedies did you use there? I mean, considering you didn't have a doctor and they're calling you...

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, he didn't give us anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I don't mean just for the flu, but there was a lot of sickness of different sorts, I know, in the pioneering. I mean, what would you do when kids were sick or parents were sick? Were there any plants or any medicines that were commonly used?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Not that I know of. I took care of a lot of them when they first started to come down. And the trouble was, try to break the temperature. We had no aspers, nothing like that them days. Nothing. So there was no woman. I think she was part Indian. She says, you know, she says you got to get them to press fire. And she says the way that we used to do it, she says, is to go to the corn crib and get some cobs of corn and put them in the boiler and put them on the boil and get them good and hot, wrap them in turkey towels, and then lay them next to the patients. And that's what I did. And I had more luck with that than anything. They did just break out, just ring in the west, and that's why they kind of relaxed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, like, tuberculosis? Was there any of that consumption?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, whoo-hoo! You want me to tell one of them stories, too?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I had that when I lived on the ranch. And, of course, it was what we called at them days slow consumption. And you just kept going downhill, and you kept working and going downhill, and you'd lose your appetite, and you couldn't eat, and you had this work to do, and you had to keep up. So my folks lived, a lot of them, in North Dakota. And so they said that I'd better come on down there. And I thought, well, I can't do it. I couldn't believe that I had it in the first place. I went to seven different doctors before I'd really make up my mind that they knew what they were talking about. But finally I had to go down there. And their medical doctor was in Bottineau, North Dakota, and their surgeon was in Rugby. So, of course, they took me on up to Bottineau. And he wanted to send me to the sanitarium up in Dunseith up in there, Canada up in there. And I said, no, not me. And my sister had a large family, and she said, well, she says we'll just put her in her room. And she says one of the girls will just take over and take care of her, and that's it. So I laid there for a couple or three months, and they had taken me to Bottineau once, and then I offered to check up. And finally the doctor in Bottineau said, well, he said she won't last until about three more days. That's all there is to it. He said the folks wanted to see her, they'd better come on down. So when they come home from Bottineau, they lived up on a large farm close to Suris, North Dakota. Well, anyway, coming home here, they sat bald and bald and snek and bald, and I said, what's the matter? I said, I know what he said. I said, I'm doomed. I can't be here much longer, but I said, what's the difference? Gotta go sometimes. And then they really did go on. Well, anyway, when they got home, they said for the folks. And they were to come the next day, which they did. That, the next morning then, I was so weak and had been, they had to carry me into the car and out of the car and into the doctor's office and all of that sort of stuff. But anyway, my brother-in-law used to come on up to my room about three o'clock in the morning. It was in the month of June, nice and light, you know. And they'd send him up there first for fear that probably I was gone or something like that. So, this morning came on up and everything looked at me and I just smiled. I couldn't talk or anything. I'd cough my head up and I couldn't hardly raise my hands. So, he went back down and then my niece came up that was taking care of me and gave me my bath. I guess she did. I don't remember that. But then really, she fed me and that. And I was weak and couldn't raise. And finally I grabbed a hold of her, a sleeve of her dress and I held on to it until I said, I want to get up. I don't know, she scared her to death. But between the time that my brother-in-law was there, my niece, I went through something that I don't know what it was. I can't tell you. I had chills and sweats and everything else. I never could explain it. There's no way to explain it. And a voice came to me and says, get up and get out of that bed and go home. So, when she came up, I just had to follow this instruction. And she said, you can't get up. And I said, you can't even set up to get dressed. And I said, no. And there's an old fashioned chair there. It took me about three hours to tell her this. Tip of bottom side up, I said, so slide the back of the chair onto my back and raise me up gradually so you get my clothes out. So, she started and then she went down to, oh, she says, I had to get just about going. And then they decided down there that, well, to do what they could for me. And I was there breathing. So, she came back up and started dressing. About three o'clock that afternoon, I woke up and dressed and I had never coughed and I've never been in bed with her since. Had the strength to walk down them stairs and everything at three o'clock in the afternoon. Now, that's unbelievable, isn't it? Yes. It is. That's a miracle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's incredible.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: That's a miracle. But that's what happened. That's what happened. And then three days after that, I went home and here I've been fooling around with it for several years. And I was okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know that voice that you heard speaking to you, is that all that it said? Huh? Was that all that the voice said just to get up and get your clothes on?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: All that, yeah. Well, the voice said, get up and get ready to go home or something like that. Live because you've got a lot to live for. That was it. But I had to do it. I had to follow those instructions. Had to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the chills and all that that happened to you then was like some kind of a turning point?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Sure it was. Whatever this was, really it turned things. There was a higher power, I'll tell you, than doctors that done that. And then my husband came and my daughters and that and we went home in three days. And then in about two years, I guess, after that, I went down there to visit again. And my sister says, let's go down into Vaudner. She says, and see Dr. McKay. And I says, okay. So they took me in there. And when he came my time to for him to talk to me, I says, I guess you don't remember me, do you? He says, no. Well, I says, Mr. Johnson's, Brother Johnson's sister that was here for six a couple of years ago. And he began to back up and back up. And he looked at me and he says, miracles will happen. And he just went and stood out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you at that time when that happened, more or less?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, I think I was in my, I don't remember. I don't keep track of those things too good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it was during the time you were in Montana, so it was about eight years.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, that was 40 some years ago. 50 some years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you had died, I suppose it would have been a real awful blow to the family.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, they was kind of prepared for it. Because I've been sick so long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were sick like that, and you didn't really want to believe it, did it really impair what you could do? I mean, get in your way a lot? Did it stop you from doing the work that you wanted to do?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, I didn't have the strength. I didn't have the strength to go ahead and do anything much. But, when I got home, it didn't take me long to get back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I...

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: One or two that I remember of. I think in this book that I gave you that Elida, she'll tell about all of that stuff in that. Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far around did the kids come to the school? Huh? How far around did the kids come to the school that she taught?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, she taught it right there on Homestead Shack. And that was right close to our place, you know. So the kids weren't ever far to go until more settlers settled there. And then they built a schoolhouse about a mile and a half from our place, more kind of in the center of the whole neighborhood, you see. I don't know, I never did have a picture of that schoolhouse. And she taught there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How serious was the water shortage?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, it wasn't... It wasn't such an awful shortage, but it was so hard, so many minerals and oil and that, that you couldn't use it. We had three wells on our place, but we couldn't use the water out for any of it, only just the water was stuck. And now, the one that wrote that book, they're still hauling water from the well that we did a mile and a half away. They built a pond to water their stock and for things like that, but for the use in the house, cooking and that, they have to haul their water. Just about the only well around the country that the water is fit to use.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did... In your visiting time, I mean the time you had to get together, was it mostly with your kin? Mostly with your relatives that you would spend your time on the homestead? Or was there a lot of other neighbors too that you would get together with?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: And I guess there's still dew out there. You know, we have their lady's aid and all that sort of stuff. And I don't know, yeah, the French, in that French settlement, they have their own church and cemetery and everything down in there. They're kind of all by themselves. But the rest of them goes into a town they call Wolf Point. That's where mostly all my people live now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wolf Point? And you were going into town back then too?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: That's the Wolf Point's on the Fort Peck Reservation on the north side. We got to cross the Missouri River every time we go to town. But they got a bridge there now across the Missouri at Wolf Point where it's popular. You still got to go across on the ferris.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what... When did this string of bad harvests start?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, see, in 1918... About 17 it began to kind of go downhill. There were hot winds begin to get so bad and that. And a lot of them toughed it out, you know. They stayed. And put their starves up and everything else. But my husband, he walked up in the field. Well, about this time of the day, it began to blow real hot. And woof. He says, I'm going up in the wheat field and see what it looks like. The way he went. Beautiful stand of wheat. Great big heads and all that. But when he brought a few back, and they were just cooked in the milk before they start to... well, about half grown. He says, this is it. He says, I'm leaving tomorrow for the coast. So he pulled out. Those that stayed... And then you see they... Get that big dam at Port Peck. Started that and after that dam got in there and got that water. Body of water in through there. They done real well. They're really wealthy people down in there now. But they're entitled to it. What they went through, I tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those years of drought were really rough?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, terrible. Terrible. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was one year after another?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, two years. My husband spent all of his money just like gambling, you know. Didn't make it one year. Try it again next year. Try it again next year. That is it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the crop a total loss?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sure is different than around the Palouse here where they never had that sort of thing.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, the Palouse country. That's something. That's a goal of mine. Ah, that. They just... And I... Once, when was that? Year before last, the crops wasn't too good down there. But this year they're having awful good crops down in East Montana again. And they're fixed now, you know. They can stand it. And then they get... they have things insured and things like that more so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During these drought years, do you remember, did they try making rain or anything like that? Did they try to make rain?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah. Uh-huh. Been down there successfully.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what they did? Did they get somebody to come in or something?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, that was... they didn't... while we were there, they tried that little afterwards, you see. I can't remember when they started this here, Fort Peck, but it was a long time after we came here. But between the time we went and up till that time, they tried that. But I don't know what they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about leaving?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I was tickled to death to get out of there. And of course, I didn't have much of a place to come to when I come here either. I had to go to work. I came in on the train at three o'clock in the afternoon. I had to go to work the next morning in order to get a place to live. This was a booming town at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this... how did it happen that you came here instead of going all the way to the coast?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, my husband went to the coast in the first place and looked around and... no, yes, yes he did. Then he came back to Spokane, went to the employment office and looked for a job. He was a blacksmith. He learned that trade in Wisconsin. And that was before they had all these modern equipments. Now, you know that they logged with horses and all that sort of stuff. And he done all that sort of work, shoeing horses and making sleighs and everything that they needed out of iron and stuff like that. So, he got a job from RuPaulin. And RuPaulin had a job here at Christmas and May Day at that time. And that's where they put him.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: This suited my husband just fine because they were just going, logging to beat the band. And this loom that was mayor of the town superintendent then, he wouldn't let anybody loaf around town if he walked down the street. And seeing somebody in the middle of the day, sitting around and that and looking at it so he wasn't interested in anything. And he'd go on up to them, shake hands with them and say, what are you doing? He'd say nothing. Are you looking for work? And if he'd say no, well, he'd just take the train out of here. He'd say this is a workman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it a working town or a working man's town?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, he wouldn't let anybody loaf around here. So, my husband worked for them for a year, I think it was, and then he turned over and worked for the pilots down at the mill down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did you think when you first came into this country? Kind of different than where you were coming from.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh my, yes. Yes, you know, there you could just look and you could see the horizon way over there, you couldn't see nothing. And here, when you look out, you'd feel like something's hitting you right in the face, all those trees and things that are close by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say you had to go to work as soon as you got here? Yeah. Why was that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Because they couldn't get a place to live. Had to go to work at a hotel down here and they said, well, we haven't got any extra room, but we sure need some help. And if you want to, we'll give you, I think the number of the room was nine, we'll give you that room until we can do better for you and your girl. Well, my husband was up here to Rupaulins in the camps working, you see, and that's the way it turned out. So I worked there for, I worked there all that winter, and the next spring, I don't know, as you remember, up to the schoolhouse there where you seen that printing office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, well, the fellow that owned that, there was a family, oh, that was the largest, large building, a family that used to live in Spokane, but they were here working, but then they wanted to move back to Spokane. And they'd been living upstairs and that was all furnished and everything. So he boarded down there where I was working and he knew I was looking for a place, so he come to me one day and he says, hey, he says, Gordon's moving back to Spokane. He says, if you want that place up there, you can move up, so I was tickled to death. I moved up over the printing office and lived there until we got ahold of this place in 25.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm surprised that housing was so tight when you came here.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, there's so many people here, many people. Everything is full the same as now and, oh, there was several Jap families living in here. Had two Jap launders that I can remember of, one down here and one up here on the corner. And people of all nationalities and the men in camp, they'd come into town. They had a strike here one time while I was working at the hotel. The people that owned it, their names were Wilson's and she done most of all the cooking. It's kind of mixed work them days. Didn't have a regular job at a certain thing. Well, anyway, we'd run the mill rights out at 1130 for their lunch. Then we had the regular mill and planer crew at 12. We'd run, was running them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Running them out? What do you mean by that? You was taking the lunch out to them?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, but they got through eating and they'd get out. So, a train, passenger train came in then at 210 and we had to get the dining room and that cleaned up again because there usually was a lot of the lumberjacks coming in to go to camp, you see, and a lot of them going out. Well, anyway, this one day, we were as busy as could be and she come to me and she says, take a look out in the office. Looked out there and there was people, all you could see is heads. And I says, what in the world now? She says, they were struck at camp. She says, there's about 300 men in here, we'll get a few. And I says, how in the world are we going to do it? She said, I don't know, we'll do the best we can. So, she sent uptown, got all the lunch, meat, and everything she could think of. There was a lot of bread and a lot of meat and a lot of everything in town. And them days, they got their meals for 50 cents, you see, 50 cents for any kind of a meal. Whether you set up the family style or what, that's what she always did. And we had to begin to rake that 50 cent piece into a sack to hold them all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you feeding people the whole afternoon?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, they were all going out on the train, you see, to Spokane. And so when the train crew came in, of course, I don't remember whether we had anything left to feed them or not, but I don't remember anything about it. Anyway, they left quite a little while before they got things straightened around again. The cooks left the pies and the roasts and the vegetables and everything on the stove and walked right away from them up in town. Plenty to eat up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this 1919?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: This would be 1920.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 1920. Do you know what they were striking for?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Higher wages, I imagine. Yeah, and then when we lived in Montana, we run into, did you ever hear of the WWs? We run into them, too. And they're the nicest people we ever had. My husband runs the town one day to hire some men. He needs some hired men off of bad. And here was some IWWs. So he brought out three of them. And the neighbors found that out and they were scared to death. They locked up everything they had. And these men stayed with us, I think, for a couple of months and worked. And were they ever faithful workers? They said, I can't understand, they said, why people don't understand us. She said, we're trying to help the laboring class of people in place of these big corporations. And they finally got so that the neighbors understood them better and found out what this was all about. And they're the ones that cleaned up these camps and things, you know. The working men had to just live in filth almost. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you had men at the place like that in Montana working for you, did they sleep in a bunkhouse or did they stay with the family?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, we had a bunkhouse for the men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know these fellows, these IWWs, very well?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes. They'd do anything in the world for us. We were haying for one thing. And they had to rush that haying out. So, of course, after they'd eat their evening meal supper at six o'clock, Pa didn't expect them to go back to work, but he'd gone out, you know. Here they'd come and work at ten o'clock and think nothing of it. Every day and every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like they were almost part of the family. Yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: At the time they were there. They were. You almost feel that way. Yeah, it's just we're... and then we needed some barbed wire one time. And Pa says, I should have about five rolls of barbed wire to fence in whatever it was. I forget. A field of some kind, I suppose. And I think they were talking about this at the... where they were eating. Looked at one another and said, well, don't buy any. We'll get it for you. Come with us and we'll get it for you. And I don't know how many spools of wire that they bought. Pa says, where in the world are you getting hold of that? He says, we got it. He says, it's throwing the ditch in the first place. He said, big corporations, it says to waste that stuff and think nothing of it. But don't let a common man have it. He says, we'll take it. They did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think they got it from?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: They were from a railroad company. That's what they got. And as a matter of fact, they built the fence that they wanted to in. These rolls of wire was just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Thrown away?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Just left there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I understand that a lot of the working people, men that worked in harvests part of the year, might work in the woods part of the year, and they were IWWs and they got around by riding the freights mostly. I was just talking to a guy last week who came from Sandpoint. He was telling me about taking the freights out to the harvests in Montana and Nebraska.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, that's been going on ever since I was a kid when I lived in Minnesota with my sister going to school there. The railroad track is right down here and my sister's place is up here. That's where you could look down there, down to the deeper. And, well, I don't know just when it started, but anyways, when harvests started out west here, or in Montana, this was in Minnesota, and people from Wisconsin and that would ride the freight trains, you know, and bum their way out west to take in the harvests and stuff like that. Oh, yeah. Lots of times. And lots of times our basement window would be gone out and them days we didn't have refrigerators and stuff like that to keep things, or separators or anything. We put our milk and cream and butter and whatever we had into the basement. They had a real nice basement, or it wasn't Samantha's Rock that made those things out of them days. We wouldn't be out of there and probably there wouldn't be a thing left. The milk and the cream and the butter and the potatoes and the meat and the work would be gone. And since we come here, they had a couple of hobo camps up here. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many men jungling up down there?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah. They don't do that anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean back then. They were being used. Would they come over to town and panhandle a little bit?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, no. They didn't bother too much. Once in a while there'd be a guy come on and he said, I'm awful hungry and you've got some little work for me to do. Can I split you some mozz or can I do some little thing for a meal? That happened quite often. But they was always willing to pay for it the best way they could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when you talk about the IWWs around in this country, the way I've understood it, of course the company was dead against him. And I think, say, Mr. Bloom, I've heard, was really against him too.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, yes. Sure. Sure. He was. Because anybody that worked for a big company, they'd better be against him, you know, because the company was strict against those guys because they wanted different living conditions for the working class of people. See, my husband has been in Caffey in northern Wisconsin many a winter. That all in the world you had was a bed made out of boards and that and some straw put in there and wool, black or dark wool, blanket on top of the straw and another one on top of you and that's all you had all winter long. You didn't have any sheets, pillowcases, any place to take a bath or anything else. Well, that's what the IWW was against. And you see, it would cost the company more to clean that up and keep sheets and pillowcases and keep some, but to keep it clean, which they had to at last. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: John, you know John DeMenis. I'm sure you know John DeMenis. John DeMenis? Oh, yeah. I talked to him up in Spokane. Did you? Oh, yeah. A couple of years ago. And he told me that Blum said, anybody with an IWW card, and this was a couple of years before you came, 17 I think. Anybody with an IWW card, he says, can't work in the mill no more. He said, you can't go in there and work at all. You're either going to go in there and work and tear up your card or leave town. Sure.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah? Oh, yeah. That's... No, of course, I didn't know too much about that IWW. It's a little city come on out to our place to work that time on the right. And they really explained things and said we were just going to keep at it until we do get things cleaned up. And they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think there was a big, say in a town like Elk River here, was there much distance between the management people like Blum and those top management people and the working people in town?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Of course, see the middle... I don't know if I'll ever put that down on the tape recorder. How to get in Dutch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Dutch with who? Huh? In Dutch with who? I just want to know the way things really were. I don't want to make things prettier or worse than they were in the past, but to be honest about it, you take a town like Potlatch, there was a big distance. Big distance between the higher ups and the working people.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, they tried to pull that here too. And of course here... There were so many foreigners, Austrians and Greeks and all that class of people. And Swedes. Well, don't record this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll tell you, if I used any of this, I wouldn't use your name in connection with it at all.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: You take these Greeks like Diamantis now and all the his pals. Diamantis had a big house right back in the alley here. I know they real well. And a lot of them. Well, during prohibition days, you know, they'd make their moonshine and all of that. And they didn't... I don't think that they drank too much, but they'd sell it to the Swedes. And the Swedes are the ones that really kept that out. Oh, I can tell you some real stories about that stuff. They were working at the hotel all that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me some about that. I'm really interested. This is the way it was really like.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, it is. And it was all new to me coming off of... Well, my folks never drank and I didn't hardly know what... My husband did because he was a lumberjack. But coming out here, it was all new to me and I'm telling you I had some experience at the hotel and one thing or another. I finally got used to it. So, we had some neighbors living up on the hill here. We didn't pay too much attention to those things. But anyway, one day she came down here. The snow was just going off on the ground. And her eyes were welled out and she said, do you want to go for a walk this afternoon? And I said, yeah, I'd mind. Where are you going? Oh, she says, you're going off the highway. I said, okay. And she says I've got to go home and get lunch for the kids and she says I'll be back when we go out that way. So, we did. And she says I'm looking for something. I says, what are you looking for? Well, she says during the night, she says I woke up. Them days you didn't have to clean up your backyard the way you do now, you know, if you had some tin cans or anything. And my garbage place up there. And so she says I didn't do anything, get up or anything, but she says I went up and looked this morning and lo and behold, she says, here's some of the bits of the decal. She says I've been hiding this moonshine in my garbage place. Great big old job she's doing there. But it was empty. And she says I looked around. She says she went down up the hill. And I said, what about if I go over this way? And she says I'll let Willa on the other side. So sure enough, when I get over here on the highway where I could see where a car had drove in that far and some tracks come from up in the woods. And he'd taken this stuff out of the car and took it up in the hills, you know, and around. So she says now we'll follow those tracks around. She says see what we can find. So we did. And we looked around. It's pretty up there. The snow was just about all gone, those little paths now and then where we could catch these tracks, you know. And pretty soon she said, oh look. And right in front of us, that great big job full of moonshine. I says now what? Now what are we going to do? Oh, I've had foolish. Well, she says I don't know. But she says I'm pretty sure I know who will want them. So we went to them and left them and one of them, he'd taken about half of these two big ones full. So we had a phone in the house then. I haven't got it now, but we did then. And she didn't have a phone up there. She says let's go on down to your place and call the sheriff, the marshal. We had a sheriff and a marshal both in town that day. And tell them come on down to the house. We won't talk to them. I says okay, by me. I don't care. So they came. The sheriff came down and he kind of hung up and had stuttered her own. And he says I wouldn't know where to find it. She says we'll go along with you. She says we'll find it for you. So we took them up there. And sure enough he did that. And he looked at them and hefted them and finally lifted the one that was partly emptied. And he sat down and put the drug between his legs like this. Took the cork out and he said, boy that's good stuff. It's all such things as I've been going on here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she do? Was she mad? Did she really think that he would arrest her?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: He then of course, he had to try to catch the guy and he knew awful well who it was in the first place. I think he's in partnership with it. Anyway, he deputized several men to lay around up in the hills here for several. Had to because she kept an eye on them. And he wasn't very well anyway. This man wasn't, we had a hospital up here then. He caught an awful cold. He had TB. And he caught an awful cold and put him in the hospital. He says he might die but he just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The moonshiner? This was the moonshiner?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know what he done with the moonshine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, this was the cop?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, this was the cop. No, I imagine they left it right there and then kind of watched it, you know. Probably took it after we left. I don't know. But he left it there. And then deputized some men to go on up there and watch. And then when I was working at the hotel, they were in... This is where they come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They always called blowing in and that kind of thing. But I imagine that, I kind of figured that if they couldn't get to Spokane or St. Mary's, it was just on a weekend or something. They'd probably be in town here. You know, getting out of the camps. Did you see much of that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, no. See, it was during prohibition days. There wasn't... Well, I guess they had a pool hall here. Couple of them. But as far as I know, there might have been a lot of it going on and I didn't know anything about it because I was working. I didn't know too much about... The only thing I know is that Gutter Quindo has a lot of these sweets and they were all pretty heavy drinkers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they just get drunk and come to the hotel and eat and that kind of thing. Is that it?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, it wasn't too much of that either. When there was... No, there was no disturbance with the drunken men or anything like that. No, it wasn't bad at all, as far as that was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you said that it took you quite a bit of getting used to. Yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, they mostly got out of town. Of course, there was no highway here then. The only transportation you had was a train. There's a freight train in here and a passenger train once a day. And there's... We used to say three crews, the men. One crew was working, one was coming into town, and the other crew was going out of town. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like for you working at the hotel? What was the work like? What you did in the hotel?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: What did I do in the hotel? Oh, I helped in the kitchen. As I say, we kind of mixed work. Helped in the kitchen and then tended to the rooms upstairs and one thing or another like that. The landlady and I kind of worked together that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was staying there?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: At the hotel? Well, see the... I don't know how many men would be working at the lumber mill. And then they had a planer, an awful big planer here. Well, some of the planer mill men was working, or rooming, in as many rooms as you had, you know, and boarding there. And they are the people that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was mostly people working in the mill, in the planing mill that stayed at the hotel then?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, those that didn't have a home with their own in town. And of course, this wasn't a company hotel. This was a mother one. The company hotel was right back of this. But there was a lot of them that didn't like to stay at the company hotel. But that was full too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think they preferred this one?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know. I don't know how that counts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I imagine that, you know, for some of them they probably want to get away from the company for part of the time since they were working for it.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: It had something to do with the pay or something. Take it out of your check or something, how that's heard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the prices the same at the two places, do you think?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Just about, I think. Here's another big old story. I don't want this published either. See, there was a big hotel right close to this tavern down here at one time. That's before the tavern was there. There was a family home where the tavern is now and then. This hotel was right above that. Well, this fellow that run it, they came from Wisconsin. We didn't know them, but we knew a lot of people and they knew a lot of our relatives from the town where they came from in Wisconsin. So that way we was pretty close. So his wife wanted to go back to Wisconsin to visit her folks and she asked me if I would, I was working then, I had to work or I figured I did. If I would kind of oversee things down there and help him out with it. And I said, yeah, I guess I would. She paid me awfully good. And so it turned out that way. She says, no, she says I'll depend on you. And I said, yeah, if I make a promise, I'll stick to it thick and thin, which I did. And so that was during the prohibition days. It wasn't long until Fritz went wild. Some carnivals came into town and he really had a ball. And then he began to bootleg. So one day he was quite open with it to me. And I said, look at here Fritz, I says, you know, I said, you're doing wrong. I said, you're going to get caught. And I said, just think of your wife and those two kids you got. I says, what'll she think? Say, he says, you ain't here to tell me things. He says, you're here to do the work. And I says, well, I'm telling you the things anyway. So sure enough, things went on for quite a while. And pretty soon, and there was a pool hall below. They were Austrians. And they were bootlegging to meet the band, I guess. I don't know, I never... they must have been. And they fed she bootlegged. But anyway, I done my work there. I usually go down there about nine o'clock in the morning and I'd be through by one o'clock. And forget about it. But anyway, pretty soon there was an elderly man that rented a little room right off from the office. And he'd come there and stay three, four days and walk around the office and up the street and down the street. And of course I had to clean his room and that and I couldn't see what in the world that man was there for. And then he'd be gone again probably a week or two or back he'd come. And that kept on for about all summer. Well, and finally the one morning I was down there and I was upstairs and looked out the window. And here I seen the sheriff and two guys searching things around out there. So I got ready to go home and went through the office and the fifth happened to be in the office. And I said, I see they're searching this place today. Yeah, I see something gets there and they haven't got anything on me. I said, good for you old Fritz. I hadn't been home until about an hour. Well, I'd got lunch for the folks and see if the telephone rang. And answered it and here's Fritz. And he says, hey, could you come on down and run this place for a while? And I says, no, I can't come down and run that place for a while. I said, you're running it good. And the detective or whoever it was stood right side of him and I heard him say, let me have that phone, I'll talk to her. And he told me who he was and that he says, we're taking Fritz in. He says, we've got it in us. And I said, no, I don't want to go down there. Well, he says, you know, he says those working men, he says, we've got to have a room to send it to and the water hot and one thing or another. Well, I says, I've never been down in that furnace room. I says, I've never been through that pool hall. And I says, you've got to go through the pool hall to get to the furnace room. Well, he says, we're taking those two guys along with us too. He says. So, come to find out, you see this old fella, he was there to watch me see the house end with the gang selling the moonshine and stuff. That's what he was. But, didn't have a thing on it because I had nothing to do with it. And they've tried there. I couldn't see it at the time, but I seen it afterwards. I had an inside room here. You came in the office here and then the rooms there and there was an inside room here, dark room. Well, one morning I went in the office there and here was two drunken lumberjacks. It was supposed to be drunken lumberjacks. And I never talked to them. When I see, if I open up a door and I seen they were drunk, away I went I wouldn't even go near them or anything like that. So, these guys, they was, had half empty bottles and talking and tried to talk to me and I wouldn't talk to them or anything. And they were detectives, you see. Oh, boy. And, did nothing do with them. And after that, why. So, I had to go on down there and he says, isn't there any of them rumors? He says that, I says, I said, I refused. I says, it's day there nights. I says, I'll stay there most of the day and see that the water tank gets hot and all of that sort of stuff. But I says, I refused day there nights. So, the detective asked. Well, he says, you come on down, he says, and do what you have been doing. He says, and I'll guarantee you. He says, the things will be okay. And they were. But, Prince, he served pretty good. Did he? Yes, he did. And then when he got out, his wife came home while he was in the pen. And when he come home, I talked to him. He said, you know, he says, when my trial came up, he says, on the judge's desk there, he says, here stood that darn bottle of moonshine. And he says that, I saw them two guys in that dark, dark room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a good story. That's a good story about the fruits of your, the consequences of your actions, you know. I mean, if you do that, what you got to expect.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: There's more to it than that, but I've still felt it tell. Too bad. Hey. I toggle in here, and he says, hey, you know, he says, I just got thinking about so-and-so the other day, and he says, I've just got to tell it to you again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's a great fellow. I really like him.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, I like George. Yeah. Yeah, he comes up here and sits. He wasn't up there last year. I don't think he's crumpled up pretty bad. Oh, that George. He can tell you more things. He's got the best memory. And he can tell it's so funny. Yeah, I imagine he'd be up to Huckleberry time again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him very well from the early years here?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes. He used to live right across the street here, when he lived with his first wife.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He really seems to have been in on a lot of things that went on around town.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, George was one of them. He knows all about it. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, that kind of story, like the story that you were telling.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: More than what they are now. Now they can take anything and bring it right out from the open and don't think a thing in the world about it. That's the way it looks to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, of course, when something is 40, 50 years old in time, then it's lost. It doesn't hurt anybody anymore. It doesn't have that kind of... There are probably things today that people wouldn't talk about today, but another 50 years would be okay.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, you know, we were talking a little bit about some of the different ethnic groups, you know, different Austrians and Italians and Greeks and different groups. And what I was wondering about that is, how much were they a part of the social life of the town? Or were they kind of separate?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: They were kind of separate. As far as I know. Yeah, they were kind of what I'd call clique, you know, kind of stick together. Yeah, there was a woman who used to live down here on the corner. She... I forget, her husband was with her at that time, but he wasn't very well and he finally passed away. And I think he had TB. I know some of her children died with TB and a bunch like that, but she used to bootleg to eat the dance. And all of that class of people, that was their headquarters. And different things like that. Yeah, they kind of stuck together. Could she make a pretty good living at that? Oh, I don't know. I imagine probably she did. I don't know. I remember one time, she was raising her family too at that time. But anyway, the youngest boy was awful, awful sick. And on a Saturday, I'd been downtown and she stopped me and she was a great woman to cry and carry on, you know. And I wanted to know if I wouldn't come and take a look at her. Georgia, he was so sick, so I did. And he ran an awful temperature, all that, but I forget what I told her to do. So I thought, well, Sunday morning I had gone down to see her. Well, I was there once again, so about 10 o'clock I went down there. The house was full then of these black men, what I call black. And her pockets and her apron was chucked full of paper belts. I think she made it all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say black men, what do you mean?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, you know, they're a dark race of people. Those Austrians and we always used to call them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they the same nationality as like Sam Pivac, Mike Buble? Did you know him?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I've heard of him. Yeah, there were some that were Serbians too. Well, there is a lot of Serbians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're different than the Austrians, right?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, there are Serbians and Yugoslavians and Austrians and Greeks and I can't name them. But there were really some niggers in here too at one time. Boy, were they the Black Hunt. There was, I believe, definitely nationalities that arisen here at one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Charlie Segura, the Japanese fellas?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, he was a nice guy. Yeah, he roomed at the hotel when I worked there. Yeah, but that was before the... Japan was so friendly with the United States that he used to have an awful bunch of Japs, older Japs, coming from Seattle every once in a while. And they'd have their suitcases with them and they had their three special rooms down at the hotel. And if the doors opened, you could see they had their tea and everything. And when those people left, I think that Charlie Segura copied everything he could think about or know about around the mill and everything and gave them all the information that they possibly wanted and took along with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What makes you think that he did that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Because you could see these stacks of books that they had, pamphlets or whatever it was, and pencils and things, where they'd been writing all of this stuff down on the tables in their room. What in the world would they be doing all that for in a small place like this if it wasn't something like that? Yeah, that happened two or three different times that I worked there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your husband get to come home a lot of the time or was it just on weekends that he'd be home? Would he come home every day from the camp?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, he could up there. The camp was only about a mile and a half up there. Oh.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Quickly up there. And then he started working down around the mill. He formed a rip track, they called it. I guess that rip track is a place where they repaired the logging cars to haul the timber or logs down out of the woods by train. And they called the trains the shea. And they'd come down on the shea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then he was here every, he could be home every day?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, yes. And then they had, there were different camps and they had tracks, railroad tracks all over up in the woods here. Different camps, one thing or another, to haul them down. But there was a strike here before we moved here. I think that probably Kenny Pearson knows something about that. They have their pickets sitting on the railroad tracks out here with their guns and they shipped a lot of the guns in here and everything else. I don't know anything about that part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, it's funny, you think about Charlie Segura. Even if he was working for Japan, that he was still such a nice fellow. I mean, he was friendly and kind to people. Yes, he was. Do you think he was well liked, too?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Seemed to be, as far as I know. I know he was an awful mannerly sort of a guy. Can't do his own business. Quite a dressy guy. Have you ever seen him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I never have. He hasn't been here for a long time, has he? Didn't he go back?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, no, no, years and years and years. Way, way, way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you, you worked at the hotel for very long?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, I worked there to the printing office. I hadn't been there many days. One evening, a rat came at the door and here's about 20 of the boarders down to the hotel. And I said, what in the world? He said, we're looking for a place to eat and sleep. I said, we haven't got a chair for you. Oh, yes, we have. And the front part of this printing office, oh, it's a large room. Largest this whole house. Longer, I think, and wider. He said, we can use that for a bedroom. I said, I haven't got the bedding or the beds or anything. Never mind. And we'll buy all that ourselves if you'll just let us come in here and you board us. They were mostly lumber pilots and they worked real hard. And they said, we like plenty to eat and we'll pay you good. So I had those men, 18 men for I don't know how long, board them. And they bought their own beds and bedding and everything. And I think I had four beds on each side. Just the alley for them to walk through. But they had their baths and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was downstairs in the printing? This was on the ground floor of the printing shop?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, no, upstairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I see.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: And the stairway was on the outside of the building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were kept pretty busy? Was that like having a full-time job to take care of feeding them and everything?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes. Yes. Yes. And then once in a while, there was two times I remember, the carpenter, Potlatch carpenter had a carpenter come to me one day and he said, hey, he says, I've got a man here. He says, I just woke from Sweden and he can't understand the word of English. And he says, he's got to have a place to eat and sleep. Well, I says, I can't have it. And he said, you better try it. He says, he's an awful nice fellow and I'd like to have him work for me. But he says, I've got to have him someplace where he can understand somebody. So it happened that I did have a place for Ed that. And he came in there and he roomed and boarded with me for a while. But one day we was eating dinner and he hadn't been eating much. And I imagine I had roast and spuds and vegetables and I had spinach. And I says, Ed, eat some of this spinach, good for you. And he said in Swedish, he says, no thank you. He says, if I wanted grass, he says, go out here and buy me a little farm. He says, eat the grass. I'm not a Swedish, I'm a Norwegian. But I can understand him pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why he wasn't eating more.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know. Food is different, you see. From what he was used to. Now that's spinach and vegetables. He wasn't used to very much of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it would be a kind of a hard adjustment for somebody fresh over from Sweden to make?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, but he was real bright. They had night school up here then for people like that. For all these Austrians and all of them that wanted to go to learn the English language. So Ed went there and doesn't know time at all, so he just picked it up just right away. Then when he left here, he went to Seattle and worked there. Finally somebody turned him on the head and threw him in the bay. The way they identified him was by his gold watch. He was really a nice man. Some of the men finally went up in the woods to work. Sunday mornings, about 8 or 9 o'clock here, they'd come on down and wait for their Sunday dinner at my house. I was kind of a mother to them. I guess that was after they began to call me Ma. They always called me Ma. Fine, old, young, and everybody. From all around here. The way that started was my youngest daughter, she's kind of a whiner. Ma, Ma, Ma. Then boarders, they began to say Ma, Ma, this, Ma, that. So it's been Ma ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they needed a little mothering? Do you think that they really needed or wanted a little mothering?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said you were a bit of a mother to them. They come in to eat your food? Yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I just love them. It felt like it was kind of home to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of those fellas, they were probably bachelors, right?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that from what you could see that that was a little hard on the men to not have families and to just be men in that way?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: It was, it was. Yeah. Oh yeah. Yeah, they were kind of lost. And then, uh... Well, I treated them as my own kids, you know, to a certain extent. I had some boarders after I come here which I didn't have room for, for more or anything else, but they wanted to stay, so I said, well, put up with it. And I said, eat what we eat and I ain't gonna fuss for you. Tickle to death.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Evenings, because there wasn't no pool halls or anything to go to them days, you know, or anything downtown much or anything like that. So we'd play cards.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Sometimes you have neighbors that's kind of curious what's going on in your place, you know. So, uh... This was during the Klu Klux Klan days.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: A bunch of them, they, uh... Had cleaned all up like they did every evening and eat their evening meal and we had it all done up and been uptown, come back and said, how about a game of whisks? I says, okay. And a bunch of us got a plain whisk, you know. And, uh... Doing nothing else. A whole thick plain whisk and that. Well, we didn't have any liquor or anything like that and we never did have it. But anyway, I guess probably the Klu Klux Klan thought we did. So while we was playing cards, my rack came at the door. And I went and opened up the door and sure enough, here stood a Klu Klux Klan all dressed up. Just didn't you see that cross over across the creek here burning? And he said, yeah, we looked at it. It didn't mean a thing to us.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: She says it's been burning quite a while. And we didn't pay no attention to it. We went on playing cards and she finally left and this was our next door neighbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the next door neighbor had a cross on his place or was it the next door neighbor that came to the door?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: The one that came to the door was my next door. That was a Klu Klux.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what were they burning the cross for?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know. I don't know. They must have thought we was doing something wrong here, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they burning it on your place?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, they wasn't burning it across. They burned it over on the other side of the creek. Yeah? And then they burned some back at the schoolhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was he trying to threaten you or anything by coming to the door like that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, I don't know. That's all she said. But, uh, I'm sure that she had in mind that we really are a rough bunch in here or something of that sort.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was to scare you a little bit? No, it didn't scare me. I mean, was it, you think it was intended to scare you?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, uh, yeah. It didn't scare me because we never done anything wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this burning cross at the school for? What do you think did he do?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, that was for warring people downtown, I suppose. I don't know, I don't know, I don't know enough about the Ku Klux Klan, what they burned the things for. And the postmistress asked me last summer if I could point out the place where they burned the cross, uh, cross. And I says, I don't believe I can. Well, she says, I'll see if I can find it. They have a homestead up on the hill here. And she goes by there quite often. I haven't seen or talked to her much this summer, so I don't know whether she's, she's, uh, found that place or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, from what you've heard, what do you think their intention was, or what their purpose was, the Ku Klux Klan in here? What was their idea for having a, you know, a group?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, I, I, I just don't know. These were southern people. You know, they tried that here a year or so ago to start the Ku Klux, down south, you know, but I guess it stopped them. I, I don't really know, but, um.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there southern people in town that were in it?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes. Of course, I think it, the most of them were relatives. I know it was a mother to this woman and this woman and, and, uh, then, uh, a brother-in-law and another sister. He was a postmistress here for a long, long time, but that was a long time after, uh, they quit dressing up and parading in the streets and things and other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they'd do that openly, they'd parade around town? Yeah, yeah. But they were hooded, so you weren't supposed to know who they were?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No, you're, you're not supposed to know, but, uh, couldn't miss this one because I know her voice so well. Well, she's dead and gone now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they ever scared, scared anybody in town?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, I imagine probably they did and, uh, my idea always was, I think that they thought that they really were somebody and doing some good. They were going to clean up some, something that wasn't supposed to be going on one thing or another. That's my idea, but I don't know. Anyway, uh, first, uh, I knew of it. I guess they'd been out one night and them days you still had to hang all your clothes out on the clothesline, you know. Well, my neighbor's clothesline here was the most uniform of a blue-clad, clam, wars and hanging on that line you ever seen. Things like that never bothered. I had no fear of anything like that because when you don't do anything that's wrong, well, you always come out to the head anyway. So, they didn't bother me too much, but I guess there was people that really scared of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I understood they really were anti-Catholic, that they were against Catholic people.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, the Catholics might not have liked them, you know.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much social life in town, uh, community type things that you...

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, yeah, they had some good lodges here. Then they had the Eastern Star and the Masons and, uh, and, uh, of course the odd fellows and the Rebekahs and all of that sort of stuff. And then they had the Moose Lodge here and, uh, they only had one church up here and that's a community church. The Nazarenes used to come in here once in a while, but they had their meetings up overhead at the store that was here at that time. Oh, yeah, they had, and then they had, uh, a large gym they used to have quite a time. They had a good band, music.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the mill going, what do you remember about the mill going out? The mill going down? The mill? Shutting down.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh. Well, that was during Depression Day, too. If I remember right, I might be wrong, but anyway, they shut the mill down and never did open it up again. They burned it, you know. And, uh, see, my husband knew the warehouses. My parents, I forget what he named one of the warehouses, Fred, Fred I believe, Pa used to call him. He knew him in Wisconsin real well, and of course he didn't live here, but he'd come up here every once in a while and talk to Pa and the rest of the men, of course, too. But he remembered Pa from the East, and that was at the time they started to build that big mill in Lewiston. And he was against it, strictly against it. He said that's where the younger people, he says, are making an awful mistake. He says, taking this mill out of here, and one thing and another. But he said they're gonna do it. He said they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said that to your husband before they shut the mill down here?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That this mill was gonna go out?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, he says when they get that one completed down to Lewiston, he says this mill's got to go. And it did. And Pa said if he has the bad, he almost cried. The old fella did. But it turned over then to the younger generations, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people have a strong suspicion that that was gonna happen, that this mill was gonna shut down?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No. No. Uh-uh. I don't think so. No, I think that the people thought, well, it'll open up again next summer. It'll open up again. And same with the plan. And then it wasn't long till they burned it, and then they began to move the company houses out. They moved an awful lot of houses out of here. Different parts of the country. And we were just sitting here by ourselves. And we could have moved to Lewiston, but my husband was just that stubborn. He says, I'm not gonna move down to Lewiston. And he says, I'm gonna stay right up where I am. That's how I'm here now. He never did want to get out of here. He loved it here. Hunting and fishing. At that time, there was a lot of his old lumberjack pals still left here, you know. Almost like a family living here. Everybody was friendly. And most of these foreigners, we call them, have moved out of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did he do to make a living after the mill left?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't think he worked. We had a lot of money. And... No, I don't know. He didn't work in the PWA or whatever you call it or anything like that. He just... Well, he, for one thing, he bought some of these buildings for little or nothing. And he started, he had a pool up there. For a long time, he run that and he kind of sold that and made a little money at it. And then he built this service station down here. And then he run that and he sold that. And then after that, he didn't do much of it. And then we owned any amount of these old buildings here, houses. Lucky he got rid of a lot of them. Most of them. So we finally made it without any help at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How rough was it? How rough was it when the town went down like that in the Depression? Those were hard years, weren't they?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: They were rough on us. Because, as I say, we had happened to... You know, when you get your own home and things like that. And my husband was a great hunter and we had all the wild birds of fish and meat and stuff like that that we wanted. And we had a little money to get down and get your vegetables and stuff like that. And then we went to work and went down to Kendrick and bought a little Jersey cow. Best little thing you ever seen. And the worst she ever had to give milk. And we had our own milk and cream and butter and stuff like that. And we had wood stoves. We went out in the woods and got our own wood and everything. Didn't cost us scarily anything to live. And then the husband to this woman that I told you that found this moonshine up in the hill. She had a sister living down on Cedar Creek. Down towards, I believe it would be downtown Southwicks. I don't know. I've never been down in there. But anyway, they had a pretty good farm there. And you couldn't sell anything, you know. There wasn't no price for anything.
