So he came down one day and he says, Ernie, let's go down to Hunt's and see what we can pick up. He says, I'd like some pork. And he says he's got lots of hogs down there. So they took an old model T Ford pickup, a piece, and away they went. And got down there and told them that they'd like to buy a pig piece and sure, you can have them. And then Hunt says, you haven't got a place up there. He says, butcher them pigs. He says, why not butcher it here? He says, we'll stir over a couple of days. He says, we'll butcher tomorrow. And then hanging the meat up and get it good and cool and you can go home the next day. So they did. And I think that, I think then hogs, if I remember, were weighed between 175 and 200 pounds apiece. And then they got 100 pounds of dried beans and cabbage and potatoes and carrots and everything you could think of that grew on the plate. They had them pick up just loaded with stuff when they come home. Cost them less than $15 a piece. So we had all that stuff picked up. No, we didn't mind it a bit, the depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did you mind the town going down? How much did you mind losing the mill? Did you personally, did that hit you and your husband really hard to see the mill disappear or did it not affect you?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh yeah, it hit everybody. But of course most people moved out, moved somewhere. A lot of, many of them moved to Lewiston. Some Spokane, some to Moscow, and some over on the coast and all over. A lot of people in this town at that time. But they couldn't budge far. He wanted to stay right here, so he did. And I don't know, in a way we didn't mind it too much because as I said we happened to have a few dollars and we liked the woods and that and we rammed all over these woods and had a good time. I still got some gold mine a little bit. I still got some raw, a little raw gold here that him and a couple of other guys mined and fooled around with that for months, a year or more. Different things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was a lot of resentment towards the company at that time for doing that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know. I don't know. But I don't know what's going to become of this town now. It doesn't look very good. Of course it don't matter to me. I can get out of here tomorrow and stay out if I want to. But I still, this is home to me. Of course I suppose if my old shack was in some other place that would be home too. I live with my daughters in the wintertime in Spokane. But that isn't home to me. That's their home. So I come back here when spring comes and spent the summer here and spent the winter up there with them. One of my daughters right now is in Yukon territory.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I thought Malcolm Anderson was such a good foreman. I thought he was just terrific to work for in the woods. Well, you know, he killed himself.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I'm wondering is what your idea is about why he killed, he might have done that.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, that I can't, I don't think that anybody really knows why Malcolm done that. You know, Malcolm was the man that drank a lot too. And here's what happened that day. The mail comes in here, all this has to do, you can get the mail about 11 o'clock in the morning. And when my husband was home, he's the one that always went downtown and got the mail, you know. Well, he got the mail that day and he run into Melker downtown. And Melker and him walked together, going home, and Melker had a bottle, of course, he bought himself a bottle. And that was it. And Pa said he just talked like he always did and all that and tell him about going home and all this. So Melker went home, Pa came home, and here, long towards the evening, why here, Heather said Melker killed himself. And that's what he had. See, I don't know what the trouble was or anything told about it, but a lot of people seem to have the impression that him and his wife had some trouble. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard this before, that his wife, it seemed like some people didn't like his wife very much either.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Oh, she was a funny woman. But as far as, see Melker had a brother that killed himself too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah. Yeah, what Emil, Emil, his name was, he's buried up here too. Yeah. Yeah, they were building this road going to Orofino at that time. And I don't know who had the contract of building the road, but anyway, Emil was working for him. And they had to use a lot of dynamite to blow stumps and one thing or another at that time right down here at Deep Creek. But anyway, Emil was engaged to a girl at that time and seems like that something went wrong. He didn't want to marry her or something. That's what I heard anyway. Well anyway, he loaded up the stump and went and sat on the stump and let them blow stuff to pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. Way to die.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, he didn't. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this many years before Melker died? A long time before that.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yeah, a long time. I can't tell you anything about a number of years or anything. But this was before Melker ever done anything like that. Was Emil a camp foreman too? I don't know that, I don't hardly think so. I think he just worked in the woods as far as I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was funny about about Melker's wife the way you remember her being?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, she was just an odd person. I've known Maggie Mae, we used to call her. She was really odd. Oh, she had three children, two boys and a girl. And what did she ever mean to that girl? You can't believe it how mean she was to Lillian. And Lillian turned out to be an alcoholic. And I think that on account of her mother, she done that. She finally got married and anyway to get away from home. And I guess there wasn't any love mixed in with this marriage or anything. And that didn't work out. Lillian just took to the bottle some terrible, I guess. I liked Lillian. And Maggie Mae had a good enough heart. And there too, but she was funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She had a good heart, you think? You say now you think she had a good heart, but she mistreated her daughter.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: See, I took care of my mother-in-law here for eight years that I didn't scare her to get out of the house. That was before they had a conlevescence home or anything together. And she had lost her mind completely in that. I was just about, well, tied up here awful, awful close. And my mother-in-law was an old-timer Rebecca. Well, beloved to the Rebecca Lodge. So someone brought it up at the lodge one day, around the evening, I guess, that they should come and visit her more. Come and help me out once in a while. Well, nobody showed up on the Maggie Mae. Maggie Mae used to come down every afternoon and relieve me. Just as good as she could be. And bring Lillian with her. And if I was ironing or started scrubbing or doing anything, they'd take over. Just as good as they could be. But as I say, she was odd. And their youngest son, you know, Emil, they named him Emil, he was killed. And he's, I think he's 15 years old. See, that was after Melker died. Maggie Mae went to work in the woods. Up in the woods here someplace. I don't know what camp it was. And Emil was up there too. And he went to jump on the side of one of those big logging trucks and slip some way or another and fell in under there and crushed him. But that was a good thing too. Maybe that sounds funny, but it is to me. That boy had started to drink something terrible. Worse than his dad had ever thought about drinking. And that's all he could think about. And I'd see him tell about how drunk he was on Saturday night and how he had a good time and what he was going to do next weekend and all that sort of stuff. So I always figured it was the best thing that ever happened that Emil passed away when he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she drink too, Maggie Mae?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No. No. Maggie, I understand she never drank. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of work was she doing in the woods after Melker died?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: I don't know whether she was what they call a flunky, that would be a waitress, or if she cooked in camp. I don't know which one she did do. But she had done a lot of that before she married Melker. That's where Melker got acquainted with her when he was running camp and she was working in camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for her to mistreat Lillian, what was the kind of thing she would do? Would she hit her?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: No. Now, this is, I will tell you the truth of it. It's very nice to tell, but they used to give parties. People like Melker that drink from being boss or something or another, because they put on some pretty swell parties. Maggie Mae could do that when she had the equipment, dishes and everything to go ahead with and do those things. And probably her kitchen and sink would be just full of dirty dishes and nothing another than the days. Take a day or so to clean things up again. And she used to take a chair and set up to the sink and get the dishwater ready and make Lillian stand there, a little bit of the thing, and wash those dishes. And before that, when she was still wearing diapers, well, made in water to wet her pants, so she caught me in the zig.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I had this.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: A man by the name of Christison, and they had to put a nice face down there and all that. And she'd been downtown shopping one day, I guess, and went home in the afternoon and dropped dead.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, Axel's a nice man. Yeah? And of course, Emil, I didn't know him too good. I think they're all three boys from a nice family. Axel lived here for many years, didn't he? Yeah. He lived in Spokane with his daughter and all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's where I talked to him, was in Spokane. Oh, were you? Oh, yeah. I talked to Axel many times up there. Yeah. He seems... He had a couple of wives, or several. He was married several times and they all died, didn't they?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, they did. Yes, they did. Didn't live long. Yeah. I didn't get to see Axel last winter. I was up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you hear about that?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well... Let's see. Well, let's start that out now. It happened one afternoon, and I've been over here to that neighbor over there in the afternoon. This neighbor across the street here was coming up the street, and she says, wasn't that awful? And I says, what? Well, she says, there's a plane crashed up here, she says, and killed a man, his wife, and a little baby. And I says, that plane, I says, that circled around here a while back? And she says, must be. And then I had a neighbor living in a trailer house back here, and I came home and she knew all about it, and she says, let's go up there. And by that time they had everything all cleaned up. And what I was told, you know, let's see if I can get that straight. They had just adopted a baby, and this baby wasn't very old, just a few weeks old is the way I remember. And they were going from out west here someplace. I can't even remember where they started from. Anyway, I believe it was a rented plane they had, and they were going back east to visit some of his or her folks. And they were on their way, and they got off from the trail or something and wound up here. And people told them not to, to be sure not to take off that way, but they did. And that's how they crashed. Now that's the story I got, I don't know. But we found the baby's bottle and a few things like that up there. That's what they said, they had just adopted this baby.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, George Schmalz told me a lot of stories about Harry Adams.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: That's another clown. That's another one. Yeah, Harry told, I guess Georgia told that hundreds of times. And my husband too, my husband is in on that with Harry. Oh, he's stuck by Harry. You bet, my husband does bet on that side too. He likes to have a lot of fun. Anyway, about a lake being up in here someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Harry really a big liar?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, yeah, he could make up the best stories you ever heard. Yeah, I don't know if he just intentionally lied to hurt anybody, but just, just stares like that. I liked Harry awful well. He used to own this house next to me here. Yeah, yeah, they had a lot of fun with our salesman. Go on, take that boat, Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are those stories that George made famous, or were they stories that were well known when they happened?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Do you think they were well known when Harry told them? Yeah. Lots of guys knew them? Yeah, I think so. I don't know. Harry might have made it up right there and then, and Paul might have helped him. He was good at that too. Oh boy, yeah, they could make it sound real good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have the Huckleberries always been good around town here?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Well, most generally. Last year there wasn't very many, and the year before, but this year, they should really be good if the frost didn't kill them. But we had a heavy frost here not long ago, so I don't know. I haven't had a chance to go out. I've been dying to go get up in the edge of the woods someplace to see if there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't it still a little early?

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, it is this year. Way up high, it'll be the latter part of August before they get ripe. I want to pick quite a few. I have a granddaughter over in Scrimm, Washington. She'd rather have Huckleberries than anything in the world. And she'll just open up a jar of them without any sugar or anything except need a quart of them. And why not have them when there's plenty of them? They're a fan. Maybe I better go and get the recipe. I believe it's four cups of sugar, two lemons, a quart of cow's milk, and a can of condensed milk, and a quart of Huckleberries. It freezes. And it's the best darn stuff you ever eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just freeze it? You just mix it together and freeze it? Yeah.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: Yes, and a freezer would be the best. Electric freezer or hand turner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hand turner. That sounds good.

speaker: Rannie (Ma) Johnson Vine
words: It is good. I made Huckleberry Pies for the Fourth of July. I got some can down in the basement. And I thought, well, I'll just fool them when they think I've been out on the hills together. So I made a couple of Huckleberry Pies.
